sections.title: What to test
items: tap_comma_slots: tap comma slots between tokens (C1-style)
rewrite_sentence: textarea with full rewritten sentence (B1-style)
One life lost per wrong screen

exercises.title: Comma placement sandbox
exercises.studentInstruction: Tap comma slots or rewrite the sentence as instructed.

items.sentence: The documentary which was produced by Channel 4 won three international awards.
items.answer: The documentary, which was produced by Channel 4, won three international awards.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Add commas around the non-defining relative clause that gives extra information.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Check if the relative clause is defining (essential) or non-defining (extra detail) — only non-defining clauses take commas.
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: The documentary, which was produced by Channel 4, won three international awards.

wrongOptions.(no comma): Missing commas around a non-defining clause makes the sentence harder to read and grammatically incorrect.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Non-defining clauses need commas on both sides — missing either comma makes the sentence incorrect.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Tap only where a comma belongs — non-defining clauses need a comma before and after the extra information.

items.sentence: We visited the museum that you had recommended so highly.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: No commas are needed — the relative clause defines which noun is meant.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Defining relative clauses identify which person or thing — they are essential and are not separated by commas.
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: We visited the museum that you had recommended so highly.

wrongOptions.(comma added): A comma here would wrongly treat the clause as extra information.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Do not add commas around a defining who/which/that clause — it is essential to the meaning.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Tap only where a comma belongs — non-defining clauses need a comma before and after the extra information.

items.sentence: My brother who lives in Manchester is a chef.
items.answer: My brother, who lives in Manchester, is a chef.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Add commas around the non-defining relative clause that gives extra information.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Check if the relative clause is defining (essential) or non-defining (extra detail) — only non-defining clauses take commas.
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: My brother, who lives in Manchester, is a chef.

wrongOptions.(no comma): Missing commas around a non-defining clause makes the sentence harder to read and grammatically incorrect.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Non-defining clauses need commas on both sides — missing either comma makes the sentence incorrect.
explanationContent.usefulTip: If the clause only adds extra detail you could remove, write commas around it; if it identifies which one, write "No commas".

items.sentence: The bike which is outside the shop is mine.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: No commas are needed — the relative clause defines which noun is meant.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Defining relative clauses identify which person or thing — they are essential and are not separated by commas.
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: The bike which is outside the shop is mine.

wrongOptions.(comma added): A comma here would wrongly treat the clause as extra information.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Do not add commas around a defining who/which/that clause — it is essential to the meaning.
explanationContent.usefulTip: If the clause only adds extra detail you could remove, write commas around it; if it identifies which one, write "No commas".
